growth_records.medium: anoxic peat soil slurry with filter-sterilized anoxic fen water
growth_records.cite: Pester et al. A 'rare biosphere' microorganism contributes to sulfate reduction in a peatland. The ISME Journal (2010). DOI:10.1038/ismej.2010.75. [PMC4499578]
growth_records.snippet: we estimated the cell-specific sulfate reduction rate (cs-SRR) for this Desulfosporosinus sp. to be 341 fmol SO4 2− cell−1 day−1. The rate was calculated by dividing the measured SRR of the turnover experiments [26.2 nmo
growth_records.notes: Cell-specific sulfate reduction (substrate uptake) rate is explicitly calculated in the paper: cs-SRR = measured bulk SRR [26.2 nmol SO4^2- (g soil wet wt)-1 day-1, from stable-isotope-probing turnover incubations at 14°C, pH 4, anoxic] divided by the natural Desulfosporosinus population size (from qPCR 16S rRNA gene abundance / 4.4 rRNA-gene copies per cell). Not a pure-culture growth-medium experiment (uncultured environmental population in soil slurry), so no growth_rate could be determined; units left as reported (fmol/cell/day) since no dry-weight conversion factor is given in the paper.
